coredata.dc:title: State of health estimation method based on real data of electric vehicles using federated learning
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Xiaoxin
Cheng, Yi
Ma, Shidian
Jiang, Haobin
coredata.dc:description: Accurately estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is of paramount importance for the development and operation of electric vehicles, as it directly impacts driving performance. Currently, there are numerous methods for estimating SOH, but only some are suitable for real-world data, most existing methods for estimating SOH use data of lithium-ion battery cells from laboratory operations to train and test. Firstly, obtaining data usage of battery from the real world takes work. Secondly, the model for estimating SOH must be representative and generalized because they are trained on separate data for each electric vehicle. Lastly, collecting data for centralized training may lead to huge data uploads This study goes beyond that by using battery data from real-world vehicle operations. This study is based on 10 in-service electric trucks over 3 years, adopting methods such as utilizing data acquisition technology from battery management systems (BMS) and other controllers, as well as vehicle networking data reporting technology, to propose a SOH estimation method utilizing a new machine learning approach based on artificial neural network (ANN) and federated learning (FL) to predict the SOH of lithium batteries in real-world scenarios. This study developed an ANN local model and compared it with the popular long short-term memory (LSTM) model, finding that the ANN model is relatively more suitable as a local model for electric vehicles. In addition, we apply the FL framework to train the model to reduce the amount of uploaded electric vehicles’ battery data. We verified the results of the model on experimental data. Meanwhile, we analyzed the model on actual data by comparing its mean absolute and relative errors. The results show that federated training method achieves better generalization in predicting SOH compared to centralized training method and another traditional machine learning models. This study provides an effective solution of electric vehicle batteries SOH prediction and offers new ideas and methods for the generalization of traditional machine learning models.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Electric vehicles
SOH establish
LSTM ANN Federated learning privacy-perseverance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Capacity calibration based on ampere integral method
SOH estimation based on federated learning
Battery SOH estimation results and discussion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by Jiangsu University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Charging capacity calculation using ampere-hour integration(b) Capacity corrected by temperature(c) Capacity of linear fit.
SOH estimation results of local model.
The SOH estimation results contrast between local model and federated model.
Architecture exploration based on vehicle networking technology.
The raw data from big data platform.
Comparison of estimation results for 8 electric vehicles between the ANN and FL-ANN.

head.ce:title: State of health estimation method based on real data of electric vehicles using federated learning

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Automotive Engineering Research Institute, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Automotive Engineering Research Institute, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Automotive Engineering Research Institute, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurately estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is of paramount importance for the development and operation of electric vehicles, as it directly impacts driving performance. Currently, there are numerous methods for estimating SOH, but only some are suitable for real-world data, most existing methods for estimating SOH use data of lithium-ion battery cells from laboratory operations to train and test. Firstly, obtaining data usage of battery from the real world takes work. Secondly, the model for estimating SOH must be representative and generalized because they are trained on separate data for each electric vehicle. Lastly, collecting data for centralized training may lead to huge data uploads This study goes beyond that by using battery data from real-world vehicle operations. This study is based on 10 in-service electric trucks over 3 years, adopting methods such as utilizing data acquisition technology from battery management systems (BMS) and other controllers, as well as vehicle networking data reporting technology, to propose a SOH estimation method utilizing a new machine learning approach based on artificial neural network (ANN) and federated learning (FL) to predict the SOH of lithium batteries in real-world scenarios. This study developed an ANN local model and compared it with the popular long short-term memory (LSTM) model, finding that the ANN model is relatively more suitable as a local model for electric vehicles. In addition, we apply the FL framework to train the model to reduce the amount of uploaded electric vehicles’ battery data. We verified the results of the model on experimental data. Meanwhile, we analyzed the model on actual data by comparing its mean absolute and relative errors. The results show that federated training method achieves better generalization in predicting SOH compared to centralized training method and another traditional machine learning models. This study provides an effective solution of electric vehicle batteries SOH prediction and offers new ideas and methods for the generalization of traditional machine learning models.

ce:section.ce:para: As global concern for environmental protection and sustainable development continues to increase, electric vehicles have become favored solutions to address the global climate issues resulting from traditional fuel vehicle emissions [1]. The development of the electric vehicle industry is also a high priority for governments worldwide. For example, the Chinese government has implemented a range of policies to facilitate the development of electric vehicles, leading to the establishment of a relatively comprehensive new energy industry chain. In 2023, significant advancements have been made in the development of electric vehicles in China. The production and sales volume of electric vehicles has exhibited continuous growth, achieving a market share of 29.8 % from January to September and 31.6 % in September. This marks five consecutive months of surpassing the 30 % threshold [2]. European governments have unveiled the 'REPowerEU' plan, with the objective of fostering energy conservation and diversifying energy supply across EU member states [3]. In 2022, the United States experienced considerable expansion in the annual sales volume and penetration rate of new energy vehicles. The sales volume reached 992,000 units, showing a 52 % increase compared to the previous year, and achieving a penetration rate of 6.9 % [4]. However, as electric vehicles become more prevalent, the prediction and management of SOH of LIBs is crucial for the reliability of the BMS, one of the three electrical systems of electric vehicles [4,5]. Accurately estimating SOH of LIBs has become more important for enhancing the safety and reliability of electric vehicles [6].

In recent years, researchers have adopted various methods to estimate and predict the SOH of LIBs, mainly divided into two categories: model-based methods and data-driven methods. Model-based methods establish models using battery characteristic experiments and aging data to determine SOH-related parameters. Ref. [7] developed an equivalent circuit model (ECM) for acquiring the internal resistance or capacity of the battery through parameter identification, thereby enabling SOH estimation. Ref. [8] proposed an electrochemical model for estimating the SOH of LIBs by analyzing the actual electrochemical reactions within the battery to assess the degradation of lithium ions and the consumption of active materials. Nevertheless, model-based methods, whether based on ECM or electrochemical models, are computationally demanding and necessitate substantial experimental data for parameter identification to accurately establish battery models. Given the practical application contexts of electric vehicles, model-based methods exhibit notable constraints in estimating SOH for electric vehicles. Data-driven methods utilize various battery data to establish SOH estimation models using machine learning methods such as extreme learning machines [9], support vector machines [10], ensemble learning [11], deep neural networks [12], and transfer learning [13]. With the increasing prevalence of artificial intelligence, data-driven methods have gained wide application in battery management.

However, data-driven methods are not without their challenges. On one hand, these methods are primarily reliant on battery experimental data obtained under controlled laboratory conditions. Nonetheless, the operational circumstances of electric vehicles are inherently intricate, and constraints in vehicle information assimilation and transmission can lead to issues of data incompleteness and high discretization. On the other hand, the computational capabilities of the BMS within the prevalent architecture of most electric vehicles may not align with those of desktop-level CPUs and GPUs, necessitating relatively straightforward network and resource-efficient models for practical applications. Additionally, the diverse operational conditions and driving behaviors of individual electric vehicles can have varying impacts on the SOH of LIBs, creating difficulties in establishing a universally applicable SOH estimation model based on conventional data-driven methods. Moreover, the extensive operational data of electric vehicles could needlessly consume and deplete bandwidth resources if all the data of electric vehicles were uploaded to the enterprise's big data platform.

This paper proposes a novel SOH estimation framework grounded in FL and powered by ANN, whereby original data is retained on local devices, and only model data is shared and updated on the server side. This approach minimizes data transmission resources, enhances the model generalization, and enables ongoing refinement of the SOH estimation model in conjunction with vehicle operations. This offers a pathway and solution for developing, optimizing, and updating SOH estimation models for electric vehicles in real-world settings. This study's contributions are as follows:
(1)
Due to the limitations in using direct real-world data from electric vehicles for training SOH estimation models, we have devised a system for directly gathering data from electric vehicle terminals to calibrate the battery capacity and extract features for evaluating a model for SOH estimation.
(2)
Local models for estimating single-vehicle SOH were established based on ANN and LSTM network, analyzing the strengths and weaknesses of the two networks and selecting network based on the current conditions of automobile electrical and electronic architectures. Furthermore many prior estimation models depended on individual battery data from local electric vehicles for isolated model training, leading to models that could have been more reliable and lacking in generality.
(3)
Finally based on the federated learning model, it proposed a novel battery SOH model federation framework that protects user privacy without sharing whole electric vehicle data. We validated the results of the model on experimental data. The model was also analyzed on the real-word data shows that the federated training method achieves higher accuracy and generalization in SOH estimation compared to the centralized training method and another traditional machine learning method.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes the relation works of LSTM network, ANN and federated learning. Section 3 describes estimating SOH by using battery data from real-world vehicle operations based on federated learning. We present the results of SOH estimation in Section 4, followed by the conclusion and future works in Section 5.

Traditional machine learning methods for SOH estimation have been widely used in previous research. These methods typically involve using statistical models or empirical formulas to establish the relationship between SOH and various input features. For example, some researchers use support vector machines (SVM) [14], decision trees [15], or regression models [16] to train and test the SOH prediction models. These methods build models by extracting physical characteristics of the battery, as well as the current, voltage, and temperature during charge-discharge processes, to estimate the remaining battery life. Some scholars have employed LSTM networks in pertinent research regarding fault prediction through machine learning models. LSTM is a special type of recurrent neural network that solves the problem of handling long-term dependencies commonly found in traditional recurrent neural networks (RNN) due to the vanishing gradient issue, and demonstrates good performance in handling sequential problems. It is extensively employed in current SOH estimation research. Each LSTM network neuron mainly consists of three parts: forget gate, input gate, and output gate [17]. Wang improved the LSTM model using singular filtering-Gaussian process regression-long short-term memory (SF-GPR-LSTM) and improved anti-noise adaptive long short-term memory (ANA-LSTM) to construct a battery SOH estimation model for the prediction of battery SOH [18,19]. He used integration of LSTM algorithm with electric vehicle operation data to extract battery pack aging characteristics and estimate SOH, while introducing the BO algorithm for rapid and accurate configuration of LSTM model hyperparameters for optimal model performance [20]. Artificial neural networks (ANN) emulate human thought processes in mathematical form, and these networks are trained on a large amount of data to obtain prediction models with certain connections and weights between the neurons. Zhang, SZ and colleagues used ANN to establish a model for the maximum available capacity of the battery in relation to factors such as temperature and battery cycle charge-discharge, for accurate estimation of SOH [21,22]. Kurucan covers state-of-the-art ANN methods, including feedforward neural network, deep neural network, convolutional neural network, and recurrent neural network, and provides a comparative analysis [23].

Traditional machine learning methods are attractive for SOH estimation because they work effectively without manual intervention, but most research assumes that the training data is sufficient and that the training and test samples must follow the same data distribution, otherwise the model may be unreliable. However, traditional machine learning methods may not be suitable for predicting the energy consumption of the entire electric vehicle network, as they typically predict the energy consumption of each electric vehicle individually. To prevent the oversight of critical factors such as driver behavior and traffic conditions, which can impact the remaining battery energy, it is imperative for electric vehicles to share their locally acquired model information instead of relying solely on their own dataset. This sharing of information can lead to more accurate predictions. Thus, the challenge lies in leveraging shared information or global prediction models to enhance the accuracy and generalization of predicting the remaining battery energy for electric vehicles.

In the burgeoning era of Internet of Vehicles, operational data of in-service vehicles can serve as our training set. However, the emergence of Internet of Vehicles introduces cybersecurity risks, particularly concerning information security. Federated learning, also known as federated machine learning, is a distributed machine learning framework, proposed by Google in 2016 [24]. The foundational principle of federated learning involves training local models on various devices within the framework, with model parameters then transmitted to the central server for aggregation. This approach ensures that the central server only acquires model parameter information without accessing the raw data. Federated learning has been successfully applied across several sectors including healthcare, finance, and transportation, and is also widely used in the prediction field. Ma used the federated learning framework, enabling collaborative training among multiple parties while protecting user data privacy, which resulted in a well-generalized global model [25]. Fekri used real residential load data to establish a federated learning framework based on the LSTM model for residential load prediction. The validation shows that federated learning-based residential load prediction not only has higher accuracy than centralized methods but also demonstrates stronger convergence [26]. Bhatt used real stroke case data to propose a federated learning-based artificial intelligence stroke prediction system consisting of ANN models [27]. The use of the federated learning framework with 5G communication channels not only improves accuracy but also increases the coding error rate and spectral noise. Currently, the automotive industry is also advancing toward high-end communication, offering an effective approach for the development of cloud-based batteries management of electric vehicles.

The electric vehicle capacity calibration method was established using actual vehicle data combined with ampere-hour integration. Through analysis of the calibrated electric vehicle capacity and driving data, parameters with significant impact on the aging of electric vehicle power batteries were identified. Multiple local single-vehicle State of Health (SOH) estimation models were established. An appropriate local model was selected by comparing the efficiency of the local models. Federated learning aims to improve the strategy for forming a high-generalization FL-ANN model. This entails aggregating model parameters from multiple local models into a central model and eventually returning them to the local model, without uploading all of the original electric vehicle data. Fig. 1 illustrates the proposed federated learning framework for electric vehicle SOH estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacity calibration based on ampere integral method
ce:section.ce:para: As the real vehicle data does not contain the current capacity of the battery, it is necessary to calibrate the battery capacity at any given time using the battery data contained in the dataset. The initial capacity of the battery is calibrated through the inverse operation of the ampere-hour integration method, and then the initial calibrated capacity is corrected for temperature. Finally, a method for calibrating the capacity of electric vehicle batteries is established through linear fitting.

The data utilized in this study were obtained from the in-service vehicles within a specific enterprise's big data platform. The selected vehicle is a pure electric light truck equipped with a lithium iron phosphate battery, featuring a battery capacity of 107.52kWh, an energy density of 117.1 Wh/kg, a rated voltage of 3.2 V, and a total voltage of 537.6 V. In addition, the vehicle's BMS utilizes a 12 V car battery as an external power supply, and it performs real-time voltage, temperature, current, and SOC detection on the battery pack and communicates through the CAN bus to send battery data to the vehicle's main bus. Moreover, the Telematics box of the vehicle's Internet of Vehicles system collects and reports these parameters on the CAN bus to the enterprise's big data platform at a sampling rate of 30 seconds. The data format on the big data platform is illustrated in Fig. 2and detailed in Table 1.

This study employed data from 10 electric vehicles within the company, spanning a period of 3 years. Subsequently, the obtained real data underwent preprocessing, involving the parsing of data types through the company's communication protocol. Data loss, anomalies, and duplicates arising from CAN communication or network interruptions during sampling were excluded. In cases of missing data, data padding is applied. For instance, in cases where mileage values are unavailable for a segment, they are substituted with the mileage values of that specific segment. If the mileage values for a segment are missing, they are completed with the mileage values from the conclusion of the previous segment. Furthermore, the data sets were divided into charging and discharging sets based on the vehicle's charging and discharging status information and sampling time.

The storage capacity and fast charging and discharging capabilities of electric vehicle batteries continuously decrease with battery aging, and SOH is a quantitative indicator used to evaluate the degree of battery aging [6]. SOH is defined based on capacity and internal resistance [28], and in this paper, we use the ratio of the current maximum available capacity of the battery pack to the rated capacity as the definition of SOH. The formula is as follows:
(1)
SOH =
C
c
C
i
× 100 %
where
C
c
represents the current maximum available capacity of battery pack,
C
i
represents the rated capacity of battery pack.

Typically, researchers calibrate the capacity of the battery in the laboratory by discharging the battery is calibrated in the laboratory through a process known as capacity calibration experiment, which involves discharging the battery at a low current, often at a rate such as 1/20 C. While this method can accurately calculate the true capacity of the battery, it is impractical for real-world applications due to the intricate operating conditions of electric vehicles. The variability in operating conditions makes it challenging to ensure discharge at consistent currents during regular vehicle operation, thereby posing a significant hurdle in accurately calculating the capacity of electric vehicle batteries in real-world conditions.

Given the high Coulombic efficiency of commercial lithium-ion batteries [29], the difference between charging and discharging capacities can be considered negligible. Therefore, this paper uses the charging capacity calculated from charging data as the indicator for SOH of electric vehicles. Considering the fragmentary nature of real vehicle data and the fluctuation of charging and discharging data, the capacity values at different charging depths are calculated using ampere-hour integration in reverse, as shown by the Eq. (2). The data reported to the big data platform by electric vehicles have a reporting period of 30 s, and the span of State of Charge (SOC) records is 0.2 %. During the charging process, the charging time of electric vehicles is not fixed, and ΔSOC is uncertain. The shorter the charging time, the smaller the ΔSOC, making c more reliant on the accuracy of SOC recorded by the BMS. To mitigate the impact of the span of state-of-charge records on capacity calculation, the charging duration should be sufficiently long. In this study, ΔSOC is constrained, and a ΔSOC of >50 % is set as a threshold to extract segments of deep charging with relatively large state-of-charge spans for the calculation of battery pack capacity. Moreover, since the sampling period is fixed and the real vehicle data is discrete, we can discretize Eq. (2) to obtain the form shown as (3).
(2)
C
c ′
=
∫
f
b
η I d t
SOC
b
− S O
C
f
(3)
C
c ′
=
∑ f b
η I Δ t
Δ S O C
where, SOC represents the initial SOC value of the charging or discharging segment, SOC f represents the final SOC value of the charging or discharging segment, during charging, SOC b is less than SOC f , and during discharging, SOC
b is greater than SOC f . Cc is the initially calibrated maximum available capacity of the battery, η represents the Coulombic efficiency, where η=1 during charging, and η∈[0.98,1] during discharging. I represents the charging current, with negative values during charging and positive values during discharging; t represents the time variable during charging, and Δt represents the time difference between adjacent time points during charging and discharging segments, and ΔSOC is the difference between the initial and final SOC values during charging and discharging segments.

Battery operating temperature has a significant impact on the electrochemical reaction processes within the battery. Zhou used the example of lithium iron phosphate batteries, high temperatures exacerbate the internal chemical reactions, causing damage to electrode materials and separators [30]. Conversely, at lower temperatures, the rate of internal chemical reactions slows down, with electrolyte viscosity increasing and ion migration resistance rising, leading to reduced electrolyte conductivity [31]. Both high and low temperature conditions have a significant impact on battery capacity. In this study, data from batteries at similar initial driving distances but under different temperatures were selected to model the temperature correction factor. Considering that battery capacity is also influenced by factors such as charge-discharge cycles, to eliminate the influence of these other factors, we selected data from the first 5000 km, during which the number of charge-discharge cycles was limited to 15 or fewer. We consider electric vehicles with an average temperature close to 25°C within the first 5000 km as the baseline, with an initial capacity of 150Ah. Using the obtained baseline capacity value at 25°C, we calculated the ratio of the battery capacity at other temperatures to the baseline capacity. Based on the relationship between this ratio and temperature, we determined the temperature correction factor using Eqs. (4) and (5).
(4)
K =
a
1
×
log
( ω )
+
a
2
(5)
C
t
=
C
c ′
× K
(6)
ω =
T
max
+
T
min
2
Where ω represents the average temperature of the battery pack during the operation of the electric vehicle, and C
t denotes the temperature-compensated capacity. T max and T min represent the highest and lowest temperatures within the charging segment.

According to Eq. (2) 1 % SOC accuracy error can result in a capacity fluctuation of 3Ah. This implies that the errors in SOC accuracy uploaded by the BMS and the discrete nature of the data will lead to different fluctuations in the calculated battery pack capacity. Hong [32] directly fitted the relationship between the number of cycles and directly measured parameters such as charging voltage, discharging voltage, and driving distance using linear regression. However, accompanying battery aging, a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film forms and thickens on the surface of the electrode [33]. At this stage, the battery capacity exhibits approximate linear decay with the number of charge-discharge ampere-hours or equivalent full charge-discharge cycles. For current daily operational use of electric vehicles, especially those with lithium iron phosphate batteries as the power source, the loss of available lithium ions due to thickening of the SEI film is the main cause of capacity decay [34]. We thus consider the decay of the electric vehicle battery pack capacity as a form of linear decay. To address this deviation in capacity calculation results, polynomial data fitting algorithms are used to characterize the decay trend of the battery pack capacity. The multivariate linear equation system for polynomial fitting is represented by the Eq. (7):
(7)
f
( x )
=
w
T
x + b

To obtain the matrix form, we substitute w T x with b, resulting in the matrix form as shown as follow:
(8)
[

]
[

Where the left-hand side represents the product of the matrix x and the matrix w. Each matrix w has a column corresponding to its matrix, resulting in a matrix related to x, which is fed to the multivariate linear model to obtain a set of parameters
y
0
，
y
1
…
y
n
in order to minimize the loss function.

When the temperature-corrected capacity c
t and the corresponding accumulated driving distance
M
c
for each charging segment are calculated, the new nominal capacity c
c can be obtained through the fitted equation. Here,
M
c
represents the current average driving distance in the c-th charge-discharge process, the nominal capacity of the charge-discharge segment is be calibrated by
C
c
= f (
M
c
)
.
The capacity calibrated using the above method for a vehicle is shown in Fig. 3.

In the process of using machine learning to estimate SOH, a large amount of data needs to be collected. For machine learning, good features can improve the performance of deep learning and thereby impact prediction results [35]. During long-term charge-discharge processes, a battery's internal resistance increases continuously, and its capacity decreases. The performance of the battery also degrades with usage. In research on battery aging, it is found that the main factors affecting battery aging include temperature, charge/discharge rates, and depth of discharge [36]. Therefore, it is crucial to identify data related to battery aging from actual vehicle data as features for subsequent models. This study will extract features characterizing the battery SOH from the perspective of battery aging mechanisms and feature engineering.

Typically, the number of charge-discharge cycles of the battery is used as the independent variable for battery aging. However, in the actual operation of electric vehicles, each charge segment is not always accompanied by a discharge segment, as users often make multiple trips after a single charge. Hence, the charge-discharge cycle count does not have much practical significance or convenience for evaluating the health of the battery pack. Instead, using mileage as a measure of battery aging provides a similar significance to cumulative ampere-hour throughput. Additionally, in the actual operation of electric vehicles, it is not always possible to maintain a constant charge/discharge rate for both charging and driving. Therefore, current and voltage are chosen as factors influencing battery pack aging.

In feature engineering, data is often classified as quantitative and qualitative, and further sub-classified into nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio levels [37]. Each level requires different control and mathematical operations. The selected mileage feature falls into the nominal level, where we take the average value of the mileage in each segment due to its minimal increase relative to the total mileage. Temperature, current, voltage, and charge/discharge depth fall into the interval level. For features at the interval level, frequency, mean, and standard deviation are commonly used for quantification. Therefore, this paper chooses the mean and standard deviation of the battery pack temperature, the mean and standard deviation of the current, and the mean and standard deviation of the voltage for quantifying the consistency of the temperature, current, and voltage of the electric vehicle battery pack during charge/discharge segments.

The consistency of temperature, current, and voltage of the battery pack is as follows:

Where ΔT, σ

represent the maximum and minimum values of the temperature, current, and voltage of the i-th charge/discharge segment, respectively.

In order to improve prediction capability and reduce training time, this paper uses a statistical feature evaluation method, the Pearson correlation coefficient, and generates a heatmap to reflect the correlation between health feature data and battery SOH. The Pearson correlation coefficient method establishes a mapping relationship between two random variables through the standard deviation and covariance values of the variables. The formula is as follow:
(15)
ρ
( X , Y )
=
cov ( X , Y )
λ
X
λ
Y
=
E [ ( X −
μ
X
) ( Y −
μ
Y
) ]
λ
X
λ
Y

Where X and Y represent two input samples, λ
X and λ
Y denote the standard deviations of the two samples, and μ
X = E(X), μ
Y = E(Y). Eq. (15) can be further expanded as follow:
(16)
r =

)
Where r represents the Pearson correlation coefficient, with negative results indicating negative correlation, positive results indicating positive correlation, and a maximum value of 1. It is generally considered that r > 0.80 represents a very strong correlation, 0.61 < r < 0.80 indicates a strong correlation, 0.41 < r < 0.60 indicates a moderate correlation, 0.21 < r < 0.40 indicates a weak correlation, and r < 0.20 indicates very weak or no correlation. The Pearson correlation coefficient method is used to analyze the correlation between feature parameters and SOH, and select features with high correlation for training to improve predictive capability and reduce training time.
The correlation coefficient between battery parameters and capacity calculated using the Pearson coefficient is shown in Fig. 4.

We use a local model based on time series, which splits the input by time. Considering the spatial and temporal distribution of electric vehicle operation, the frequency of vehicle use is higher on holidays than on weekdays. Moreover, the battery wear and tear is increased. Therefore, this article extracts each feature by cycle after each charge.

In order to simultaneously ensure the accuracy and efficiency of SOH estimation, this study establishes two single-cell models, namely an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), using features selected through the Pearson correlation coefficient. For training and validating the deep learning models, the input data is divided into a training set comprising 80 % of the data, used to train the nonlinear relationship between battery pack capacity degradation and health characteristics, and a validation and testing set comprising the remaining 20 % of the data, used to verify the accuracy and generalization ability of the model. To evaluate the estimation accuracy of the models, a fixed random seed is set to ensure the initialization of weights for the deep learning models under the same conditions. During the training process, the models may encounter overfitting problems, which are addressed by incorporating regularization terms and dropout layers to prevent overreliance on specific neurons in the machine learning models.

This study develops an Electric Vehicle battery SOH estimation model based on an ANN. ANN is based on the principles of biological neural networks, mimicking the organizational structure and some activity mechanisms of the human brain neural system [21]. Through a large number of interconnected simple-function neurons, it forms a complex adaptive nonlinear dynamic system. As shown in Fig. 5, the ANN consists of input layer, hidden layer, and output layer. The leftmost layer is the input layer, where each segment computes the input values by
∑
i = 1
n
w
ij
x
i
+
b
j
and activates through an activation function, resulting in the input values for the middle hidden layer. Each neuron in the hidden layer receives the output values from the previous layer as input, and then outputs through an activation function to produce the final output Y.

As depicted in Fig. 5, the input layer comprises two neurons, while the output layer consists of only one neuron. The specific number of neurons in the hidden layers is indeterminate and will be determined at a later stage. The output of the hidden layer is computed based on the connection weight
w
ij
and the bias b
i between the hidden layer and the preceding layer.
(17)
H
j
= f
(
∑ n
i = 1
ω
i j
x
i
+
b
i
)
, j = 1 , 2 , … , l

Where l is the number of the hidden layer neurons and f is the activation function of the hidden layer. The formula of final output of ANN is as follows:
(18)
O
k
= f
(
∑ n
j = 1
ω
j k
H
j
+
b
j
)
, k = 1 , 2 , … , m
Where m is the number of the output layer neurons, ω
jk and b
j are the connection weight and bias between the hidden layer and the output layer, respectively.

LSTM addresses the issues of vanishing and exploding gradients and provides better modeling and forecasting for time series data through well-designed gates to update and store unit information[38]. The LSTM neural network structure, as shown in Fig. 6, consists of three gate control systems: the forget gate, input gate, and output gate. Training the input parameters of the LSTM model determines which information to remember and which to forget. The update process is described by Eqs. (19)-(21). The final output at step t is computed by Eqs. (22) and (23).
(19)
f
t
= δ
(

)

where δ and tanh are activation functions, f, i
t , O t represent the output vector of the forget gate, input gate, and output gate at time t respectively, x
t represents input vector at time t, h
t represents the final output at time t, W

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOH estimation based on federated learning
ce:section.ce:para: Based on traditional machine learning, a local estimation of SOH of dynamic batteries is constructed. Furthermore, a federated learning model has been designed to aggregate the parameters of global models by establishing a central model, ensuring that the model can adapt to the heterogeneity existing between different vehicles and synchronize among local models.

The workflow of federated learning is as follows: first, assuming there are N separate electric vehicle nodes in the server, i.e., N individual vehicle nodes. In this study, 10 electric cars from a certain enterprise are selected as the research objects, so N = 10, and can be denoted as V1, V2, …, V10. Each electric vehicle has its own battery dataset Xk = [Xk i,j] n×m, where k= 1,2, …, N, N is the quantity of data in the dataset, and m is the number of features contained in each data, i.e., the data dimension.

The usual method is to integrate all the datasets, assuming X=X1∪X2∪…∪XN, and train the model using X. However, due to various policies and regulations and the privacy of each vehicle owner's data, these data often cannot be completely shared publicly, which brings difficulties to general model training.

Federated learning can effectively solve this problem. In the federated learning model, the central server first initializes the model and assigns it to each individual vehicle node. Each vehicle node trains the model based on its own battery data. For example, in the t-1th iteration, vehicle Vk uses data Xk to train the model, resulting in a local optimal model Gk(t-1), which is then uploaded to the central server without providing its original data Xk. Finally, the N local optimal models G1(t-1), G2(t-1), …, GN(t-1) are obtained through the federated averaging algorithm to obtain a universal global model G(t-1). At the same time, in the next iteration t, the central server assigns the previously obtained global model to iterate over each vehicle node, continuing to update the local and global models until convergence of the iteration count or the global loss function. The complete iterative process is as follow:

Step 1: Initialization stage. Given the number of individual vehicle nodes N, iteration count, learning rate, and initial global model parameters. Step 2: Local model training stage. In each iteration, the loss function of each individual vehicle node is as shown in Eq.(24).
(24)
S (
G
k
( t − 1 ) ) =
1 m
∑ m
i = 1
∑ n
j = 1
(
x
i , j
k
−
x ˆ
i , j
k
( t − 1 )
)

Where L represents the loss function, indicating the error between the predicted value and the actual value of the trained local optimal model parameter set G
k (k) in the (t-1)-th iteration.
x
i , j
k
represents the measured sample label value Vk corresponding to the training dataset Xk of the individual vehicle,
x ˆ
i , j
k
( t − 1 )
represents the predicted label value of the individual vehicle in the (t-1)-th iteration, and m and n are the number of samples in the dataset Xk.

Based on Eqs. (25) and (26), the local model is updated as follows:
(25)
G
k
( t )
=
G
k
( t − 1 ) −
η
k
∇ S (
G
k
( t − 1 ) )
(26)
Δ S (
G
k
( t − 1 ) ;
B
k
) =
1
m n
∑
m n
k = 1
∂ S (
G
k
( t - 1 ) )
∂
G
k
( t - 1 )
Where η
k represents the learning rate of individual vehicle Vk; B
k refers to the mini-batch of data samples randomly drawn from the dataset Xk. By using mini-batch samples to compute the gradient values and update local models, computational speed can be improved; |B
k | represents the number of data samples in B
k , and ∇ represents the gradient symbol.

Step 3: Global model update phase. According to the Federated Averaging algorithm FedAvg proposed by Google McMahan et al. [39], the global model is aggregated and updated, as shown in Eq. (27), and the global model loss is calculated based on the global loss function, as shown in Eq. (28).
(27)
G
m
( t )
=
1 N
∑ N
k = 1
G
k
( t )
(28)
S (
G
m
( t )
) =
1 N
∑ N
k = 1
S (
G
k
( t − 1 ) ) =
1
N m n
∑ N
k = 1
∑ m
i = 1
∑ n
j = 1
(
x
i , j
k
−
x ˆ
i , j
k
( t − 1 )
)
Step 4: Repeat steps 2 and 3 until the global loss function converges, or reaches the target number of iterations, or achieves the target accuracy, and designate the final global model as GM.

For local model selection, a knowledge approach similar to traditional machine learning was utilized, as discussed in Section 3.3. Each model was reassessed through the unfreezing of nodes to identify the maximum number of substantial neural nodes within multiple layers. In this study, only the fully connected layers present in each model were utilized. However, the nodes were not frozen at this layer, as the information regarding the training data is not available at the server level. As a result, the central server determined the average number of active neural nodes based on a threshold, to establish the global threshold (MAVG)。

Subsequently, models exceeding the global threshold were selected and sorted based on their deviation from the global average. The client manager on the central server used this specific round's model sorting list to select the best model based on the sampled client count, addressing the issue of insufficient generalization in traditional learning due to the heterogeneity of datasets.

In the SOH estimation process, the MAE, MSE and RMSE are employed for the critical evaluation of the performance of the proposed FL-ANN model. The MAE is the average of absolute errors. It can better reflect how the estimated error value is individually weighted equally around the actual value (the positive and negative offset each other). The expression for the calculation of the MAE is shown in Eq. (29).
(29)
MAE =
1 N
∑ N
i = 1
|
C ˆ
( i )
−
C
L
( i )
|

The MSE is used to evaluate the expected square value of the difference between the estimated value and the true value of the residual capacity. The MSE is calculated by squaring the difference between the actual and estimated values and then averaging the sum. The expression for the calculation of the MSE is shown in Eq. (30)
(30)
MSE =
1 N
∑ N
i = 1
(
C ˆ
( i )
−
C
L
( i )
)
2

The RMSE is calculated by the square root calculation of the evaluation ratio for the square of the deviation to the number of samples m to indicate the degree of dispersion. The deviation is obtained by the residual calculation between the actual value (
C ˆ
( i )
) and the estimated value (C
L (i)). The RMSE is used to measure the deviation between the observed value and the true value. The expression for the calculation of the RMSE is shown in Eq. (31).
(31)
RMSE =
1 N
Σ
i = 1
N
(
C ˆ
( i )
−
C
L
( i )
)
2

All experiments in this paper were conducted in the Python 3.7 environment, with data preprocessing implemented using third-party libraries such as pandas and numpy. Machine learning and federated learning were mainly implemented using third-party libraries such as tensorflow and pytorch. The hardware used included a desktop-level Intel Core i5–12400 f processor with a base frequency of 2.50 GHz, and an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070ti desktop-level GPU. All experiments in this study were conducted in the Python 3.7 environment, with data preprocessing implemented using third-party libraries such as pandas and numpy. Machine learning and federated learning were mainly implemented using third-party libraries such as tensorflow and pytorch. The hardware used included a desktop-level Intel Core i5–12400 f processor with a base frequency of 2.50 GHz, and an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070ti desktop-level GPU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery SOH estimation results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: This chapter experiments to verify the improvement in predictive accuracy and the feasibility of the method proposed in this paper. The experiments were conducted in two parts: local model and federated learning model. Prior to training the models, data preprocessing and feature engineering were performed to standardize the dataset, in order to mitigate the negative impact of differences in scales between different features and labels. To validate the feasibility of the local model, data from a specific vehicle was selected as the target for simulation, and LSTM and ANN models were separately established for training. The dataset was split into 80 % for training and 20 % for testing. In the current research on SOH estimation based on machine learning, whether it is based on laboratory data [38] or real vehicle data [20], researchers use the same battery data or consistent operating conditions as the training and testing sets. Due to the variability of actual operating conditions of electric vehicles, such validation methods cannot fully verify the model's generalization. The local model experience serves to demonstrate the issue of generalization in traditional machine learning, the data from one vehicle was used as the training set, while the data from another vehicle served as the testing set. Finally, in order to validate the enhanced generalization of federated learning compared to traditional machine learning, data from 10 vehicles were utilized. Specifically, 80 % of the data from each individual vehicle was designated for training, with the remaining 20 % allocated for testing, to collectively construct the model.

The study initially established models for individual vehicles, following the common approach in current research. It was observed, as shown in Fig. 7(a), that both the LSTM and ANN models exhibited satisfactory performance, the estimation results of the SOH model established based on LSTM presented by He [20], with RMSE and MAE values of 7.552 % Ah and 1.521 % Ah, respectively, are very close. Therefore, the local model demonstrates excellent accuracy in addressing single-vehicle SOH estimation problems. Without altering the model parameters, Fig. 7(b) and the table illustrate the performance comparison between the LSTM and ANN models for the 10 individual vehicles. Comparatively, while maintaining accuracy, the speed of the ANN model was several times that of the LSTM model. This was attributed to the relatively simpler model structure of the ANN, which reduces its usage of computational resources. As electric vehicles are currently transitioning towards domain controller architecture and local computing power is limited, it is crucial to select a model that does not heavily occupy resources as the local model. In this regard, the study will adopt the ANN model for subsequent research and validation.

It was observed, as shown in Fig. 7(c), that traditional machine learning does not possess reliable generalization when trained on data from different vehicles. This is due to the fact that the ANN model can only learn the features of the training dataset during training, and the heterogeneity of operating conditions for each electric vehicle results in the features of the training set being unable to adapt to all vehicle types, thereby causing the model to fail on different electric vehicles.

The federated learning model experience compares the predictive performance of a single participant in local model training with the collaborative multi-party training of a federated learning model. Using data from 10 vehicles, a federated learning model was established, and aggregation and iteration were performed to compare the predictive results after federated learning iteration with those under the local learning approach, in order to validate the superiority of federated prediction in improving generalization. The comparison of RMSE and MAPE prediction metrics for all participants is illustrated in Fig. 8 and Table 2, under the local learning method, there is a large variation and significant span in the predictive errors of MAE and RMSE, attributed to the heterogeneity of features among each vehicle. From Table 2, it can be observed that the federated learning model, constructed through the participation of multiple parties, exhibits higher predictive accuracy in forecasting the SOH of multiple electric vehicles compared to the single local model trained on the collective data of individual electric vehicles. The highest performance improvement is evident in vehicle 1, with a reduction of 4.948417 % in RMSE and 4.098 % in MAE. Although vehicles 5 and 6 show a relative increase in MAE and RMSE compared to the local model, the overall performance of MAE and RMSE remains below 0.6 %. The utilization of a federated learning strategy in this paper demonstrates that, while preserving the privacy of local datasets, the collaborative training of charging load prediction models from multiple participants has significantly enhanced the predictive accuracy and effectively improved the generalization of the model, primarily due to a richer and more diverse training dataset.

Considering the progress in vehicle networking technology, as well as the widespread adoption of big data technology and Over-the-Air (OTA) updates in diverse vehicle models, it is evident that computationally intensive tasks can be offloaded to the cloud. In addition, models centrally aggregated from the cloud can be adjusted via OTA updates to effectively align with each electric vehicle's Battery Management System, thereby completing the loop of federated learning. To achieve this envisioned architecture, the overall framework and steps are outlined in Fig. 9 as follows:
(1) Deployment of local models on each electric vehicle for training based on historical BMS data.

(2) Upload of the model parameter data from the local training models on each electric vehicle to a big data platform, where a centrally aggregated model is established for federated learning to produce the optimal estimation model.
(3) Modification of the corresponding BMS software based on the optimal model, which is then imported into the OTA platform.
(4) Remote updates executed by the OTA platform to update the local model on each vehicle.

This study applies federated learning and traditional machine learning to estimate SOH of electric vehicles. The sample data is derived from the operational data of 10 pure electric vehicles over the past 3 years. Based on historical charging data, the battery pack's charging capacity is calculated using the ampere-hour integration method, and coefficient for temperature correction is utilized to eliminate the influence of environmental temperature. The linear polynomial fitting algorithm is employed to qualitatively characterize the capacity fluctuation trend. Based on the battery aging mechanism, health features related to user behavior are extracted, and the Pearson coefficient is used to analyze the nonlinear coefficients of capacity and health features to distinguish the heterogeneity of user behavior. The first local model for SOH estimation is based on LSTM network and ANN, while the second central model for SOH estimation is based on federated learning. Using three performance metrics MAE, MSE, and RMSE to compare the accuracy and practicality of LSTM and ANN, federated learning, and traditional machine learning, the results show that federated learning can effectively improve the generalization of traditional machine learning.

Although the proposed model has advantages in addressing SOH estimation based on real vehicle data and model generalization, there are some limitations. Firstly, in this study, all the datasets used were exported from a big data platform, and true local training on real vehicle side was not realized. Therefore, this study proposes an architecture concept based on big data technology and OTA technology that can truly apply the model in practical scenarios. Furthermore, establishing personalized models by modifying the aggregation process of the federated learning model can further improve the generalization of the model under heterogeneous data conditions.

The use of personalized federated learning based on meta-learning to address the generalization issues of traditional machine learning in estimating SOH of electric vehicles will be the focus of our future work. The meta-learning approach aims to provide a tailored personalized model architecture for each local model, while leveraging the relationships among local models to enhance the performance of personalized models. This entails establishing similar personalized models for relevant local models, thereby improving the generalization of the model.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by Jiangsu University.
